speaker: interviewer
text: --is Martin Perlmutter on March 6th, 1995.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to ask you now, to what do you attribute your survival?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the most interesting question I've ever been asked. I would say my optimism. I never felt that I'm going to die. I was always ready to find a way out of the dilemma. I told you I escaped twice. I fought the elements. I was lucky. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was lucky, but I would say that the the the main reason I'm alive is that I believed that I'll survive. I had faith, not necessarily in-- I don't know how to describe it-- but I never felt that this is going to be my last day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think about the Shoah, the Holocaust, often?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have awful dreams, often. Most of my dreams are running. I'm running-- running away, running to hide. And I can't-- I can't get where I want to go. And they are awful dreams.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even now? [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, very often.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why did you want to tell your story, which you have repeated several times now for different agencies?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it's important. I won't be here much longer. I'm going to be 77 years old soon. And there are people in the world-- so-called intellectuals-- are trying to deny the-- what happened during the Holocaust. And I feel strongly that if I can contribute with my testimony to assure the interested ones that it did happen. My family died-- my mother, my sister, my brother, my sister's child, all my family from my mother's side, except one sister that is close to 100 years now and lives in a home in Israel and her one son survived that is a doctor in Haifa. His wife is a doctor. All three children are doctors. One of them is a genius. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I can only think of if the Holocaust did not happen, how many people, how many [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] geniuses, the world have lost. All the children, over a million children killed without guilt. I've been once to the University of Judaism. And there was a professor teaching Holocaust. And the first statement he made was, we'll talk about the Holocaust, but we won't talk about the years from 1939 to 1945. And as a Holocaust survivor, I thought, he must be nuts. But he was brilliant. And he taught me the problems that the Holocaust created in the world for the Jews, for the non-Jews. Christians have a dilemma that they-- that they killed people in the image-- that were born in the image of God, and 6 million of them perished. And they cannot justify that, what was done. The religious orthodox, strictly Orthodox Jew, has a problem with the Holocaust that's-- they say, [NON-ENGLISH] because of our sins, we were punished. Now you can't justify the million children being killed because of their sins. They had no sins. So the Holocaust brought on many problems for the world to cope with. And my testimony now is geared to tell whoever is interested in the future what I lived through and what I saw.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way do you think your experiences during the Shoah have affected your outlook, your behavior, your philosophy?

speaker: interviewee
text: I consider myself a pretty stable person. I don't know whether the Holocaust made me better or worse. I don't live in the past. I dream about it, but my life is [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] well-adjusted, as I would say. So whatever happens in my life is not necessarily due to what I lived through. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I would consider myself as well-adjusted a person as people that never went through the Holocaust. I don't know what to contribute that to, but that's the way I feel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were any of the perpetrators of these Holocaust atrocities brought to justice, to your knowledge-- the ones that you saw and came in contact with?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know that Warzok was caught. He was jailed. My friend, [? Mikail ?] Borohowicz was a witness in Germany against Warzok. I gave testimony in New York to the German Consulate, and we-- against Warzok. From what I heard later on that he disappeared from jail in Germany. He lives someplace. I have no idea where. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You've been married--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to your husband for 35 years.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, and I really feel that I'm very, very fortunate. I met him the first day that he came to America. He came to my father's home because we are first cousins. And I know the struggles that he had here, and they were dreadful. And I had many struggles myself. And I don't think there's a day that we don't feel lucky to have each other and to have made a life for ourselves after the hardships, especially his. I know when he talks about his nightmares. He'll get up very often at night. And he has to wash his feet with hot water, because he's just running away from the Nazis. And he still struggles with memories. But his whole approach to life-- his whole objective for living is to live for today and to make the best of the day. And he's very optimistic and a very strong person. And he did tell you some of his sports. He forgot to tell you that he loves bridge. And he plays a great deal of bridge. He has tremendous amount of interests. He has painted all the paintings in our home. He's painted paintings for our children, although he never really wants to hold a brush again because that's what he did for a living. And he's made a new life for himself in Florida. Also, in testimony, I must say that our two oldest grandchildren absolutely worship and adore him. And they are living as third generation with the feeling that you cannot let it be forgotten by anybody. I'm holding something that I hope to send to Spielberg that my granddaughter has written in school. She very often does book reports and writes about the Holocaust. And that's in tribute to him. But after we're gone, our children know, and grandchildren are actively interested in what has happened. If I have a moment, I'd like to read the last paragraph of this letter. It really is taken out of context and may be a little weak without following all that she says about her grandfather because it is about him. But she says, 'The most important way to keep the denied past alive is to remember. Remember it well and never forget. Teach others, and learn as much as you can about this unfortunate but most important era in our history.' And, well, as I say, I can't read it all. But she lives in a little town in New Hampshire where there are token Jews. And she lets all her teachers and friends know about Jewish holidays, about her grandfather, about his past, about his bravery, and also about what an incredibly marvelous person he is.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture represents my sister and myself in 1921. I was not quite 3 years old in this picture. And this is the only picture that tells me that my mother was not 100% a truthful person because it says here, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. That means, these are my two beautiful children. Well, I don't look beautiful in this picture. I can tell you that much.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: This represents my hometown, my home in Sasow. And it-- in the window, you see my grandmother. I'm on the left, my sister next to me, my uncle and his wife, [NON-ENGLISH], my mother and my brother, Max. The signs you see here tells you what kind of business we were in. We were selling liquor. This says, soda, water, coffee, tea, and sweets. And that's in front. And I made those signs when I was a youngster. [INAUDIBLE] In the window, you see my grandmother, Leah. And right next to her-- I mean, the first one on the left, that's a picture of myself. This represents the cousins, my brother Max on the right standing, myself next to him, my cousin, all the cousins, and my sister sitting. The only one that survived is the one next to me, my cousin that I mentioned in the testimony who's a doctor in Haifa, Israel. All the others were killed by the Nazis.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: This photograph represent a Purim costume party in my hometown. At the extreme right is my brother, Max, that was killed in Zloczow. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] My grandfather, Jakob Sperber, father of my mother, in Buchach, Poland. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Taken in 1932. 1932. The year? 1932-- the year of this-- when this picture was taken is 1932. This picture represents my sister, Adele, with her husband, Shmuel Shalit. In 1938, they were both killed in [NON-ENGLISH], 26 days in Adar. Those are my three aunts, sisters of my mother. The one on the right is Yetta. Standing up is Luba. And the one on the left is Genia that survived and is in a home now in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old is she now?

speaker: interviewee
text: She is almost 100 years old now. The other two aunts were killed. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a picture taken of my teacher, Nachimowicz, in Zloczow. He's the man that was successful to run-- to run away with me twice, once in the woods of [NON-ENGLISH], and his next time from Janowska camp. He was caught and thrown to the dogs. The dogs ripped him apart. This represents the Bnei Akiva organization that I was member of. I am in the second row with the hat with a-- with a white collar next to that young lady with the curly hair. My brother is in the first row, the third from the right, the one with a beret on his head. The man to his left with a hat is Yitzhak Rafael. Later, he became Minister of Religious Affairs in Israel. This is a picture of Sasow organization, Chovevei Tzion, which my father was part of. And he is seated to the left of the man standing on the right with a beard. He was sitting in the middle there, the second from the left. At the second on the right in the second row seated is [NON-ENGLISH], who became a famous poet. All the others are dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photo of the Hebrew school in my hometown, Sasow. I am seated in the front with a white collar, the smallest of everyone. And there are a few other people to be pointed out. One that's holding a child on the left-hand side with a hat on is Menachem Rafael. And next to him, to the left, is Yitzhak Rafael, who became later the Minister of Religious Affairs in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Date of picture. Date of picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in 1925. [PAUSES FOR 15 SECONDS] This picture represents some of my work that I did in Zloczow when I worked for the Officers' Club. And it depicts realism in the Soviet Union. It was a very large painting made on plywood. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] This represents a group of people that worked in the [NON-ENGLISH] when we were carrying the water to the radio station in 1941. On the left, myself. Next to me is my friend, Hannah Felig. Both of us-- it was visible the armbands that you had to use with a Jewish star on it. I'm the only-- the sole survivor in this picture. The others were all killed by the Nazis.
